chore(beads): close bd-1nq (conformance CI), advance bd-uah (perf comparison)

Update issue tracker to reflect conformance CI integration and
performance workstream progress:

- bd-1nq (integrate extension conformance harness into tests + CI):
  close — harness tests wired into cargo test default set, CI workflow
  updated with generated/negative/scenario test jobs and artifact uploads
- bd-3dxz (memory profiling and 1-hour stress test): timestamp refresh
  from ongoing profiling work
- bd-uah (compare extension performance vs legacy pi baseline): advance
  open -> in_progress — performance comparison benchmarks under active
  development

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1no3","title":"Phase 3: Differential Test Runner (compare TS vs Rust output)","description":"# Phase 3: Differential Test Runner (compare TS vs Rust output)

## Purpose
This is the CORE of the conformance system. The differential test runner:
1. Takes an extension path and a mock spec
2. Runs it through the TS runtime (via Bun subprocess)
3. Runs it through the Rust runtime (via library call)
4. Compares the outputs
5. Reports PASS (identical) or FAIL (with diff)

## Why A Runner (Not Just Tests)
Individual test functions are fine for small numbers of extensions. But we have 200+ extensions to test. The runner automates:
- Extension discovery from the validated manifest
- Mock spec selection (per-extension or default)
- Parallel execution (both runtimes concurrently)
- Structured output comparison
- Report generation

## Runner Architecture

```
ConformanceRunner
  |
  +-- for each extension in manifest:
  |     |
  |     +-- Run TS harness (Bun subprocess) -> ts_output.json
  |     +-- Run Rust harness (library call) -> rust_output.json
  |     +-- Compare(ts_output, rust_output) -> diff_result
  |     +-- Record result
  |
  +-- Generate report: {total, passed, failed, skipped, diffs: [...]}
```

## Comparison Strategy
Not just byte-equality. Semantic comparison that:
- Ignores key ordering in JSON objects
- Handles numeric precision differences (f64 vs TS number)
- Normalizes string whitespace where appropriate
- Groups differences by category (registration, event, hostcall)
- Provides human-readable diff output

## Output
- Per-extension: PASS/FAIL with detailed diff
- Aggregate: summary report with pass rate per tier
- Machine-readable: JSON report for CI
- Human-readable: markdown report for review","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:20:50.307953690Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T17:34:53.485560824Z","closed_at":"2026-02-05T17:34:53.485496845Z","close_reason":"Differential test runner implemented in tests/ext_conformance_diff.rs. Runner: discovers extensions from VALIDATED_MANIFEST.json, runs TS oracle (Bun + load_extension.ts), runs Rust runtime (QuickJS), compares outputs with diff_snapshots(). All 60 official extensions pass.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1no3","depends_on_id":"bd-3odv","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:20:50.327770840Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":311,"issue_id":"bd-1no3","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"DESIGN DECISION: Why Rust integration test (not standalone binary)?

1. CARGO TEST INTEGRATION: Each extension as a #[test] function = automatic parallelism, filtering, and CI integration.
2. LIBRARY ACCESS: Can call Rust extension runtime directly without subprocess overhead.
3. EXISTING PATTERN: conformance_fixtures.rs already uses this pattern with fixture_test! macro.
4. REPORTING: cargo test --format json gives structured output for CI.

The TS side runs as a Bun subprocess because we cannot embed the TS runtime in Rust. This is fine -- the subprocess produces JSON on stdout, which is trivial to parse. The overhead is ~50ms per subprocess spawn, acceptable for 200 extensions.","created_at":"2026-02-05T07:27:17Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1o6l","title":"Extension load-time benchmarks (all 60 official, TS vs Rust)","description":"# Extension load-time benchmarks (all 60 official, TS vs Rust)

## What
For every official extension, measure load time in both runtimes. The differential runner already captures load_time_ms -- this task aggregates and analyzes the results.

## Analysis
- P50, P95, P99 load times for each runtime
- Per-extension comparison table
- Identify outliers (extensions much slower in Rust)

## Target
- P99 < 100ms for Rust
- Rust <= 2x TypeScript for any extension
- Rust median <= TypeScript median

## Output
- Benchmark report: tests/ext_conformance/reports/load_time_benchmark.json

-{"id":"bd-3dxz","title":"Memory profiling and 1-hour stress test (10+ concurrent extensions)","description":"# Memory profiling and 1-hour stress test (10+ concurrent extensions)

## What
Load 10+ extensions simultaneously, continuously fire events for 1 hour, verify:
- No memory leaks (RSS growth < 10% over the hour)
- No deadlocks
- No panics
- Consistent event dispatch latency (no degradation over time)

## Method
1. Load all 60 official extensions
2. Fire events in a loop at 100 events/second
3. Monitor RSS every 10 seconds
4. Monitor event dispatch latency continuously
5. After 1 hour, verify all metrics within bounds

## Target
- RSS growth < 10% over 1 hour
- No crashes/panics
- P99 latency does not increase by more than 2x over the hour
- All extensions still responsive at end of test

- Single-threaded scheduler loop that is reproducible under fixed inputs.
- Trace IDs for scheduled items (for deterministic logs and replay).

- Ordering preserved: microtasks always drain before timers.
- Tie-break: FIFO by enqueue time and sequence id.
- No hidden randomness or time-based ordering.

- Unit tests for queue ordering and tie-breaks.
- Conformance fixtures for microtask/timer ordering.

 {"id":"bd-9sa","title":"Implement asupersync HTTP client wrapper","description":"# Implement asupersync HTTP client wrapper

## Goal
Create a functional HTTP client in src/http/client.rs using asupersync APIs.

## Current State
src/http/client.rs has:
- Client struct (empty)
- RequestBuilder struct with method/url/headers/body
- RequestBuilder::send() returns Err("asupersync HTTP client not yet implemented")
- RequestBuilder::json() silently ignores serialization errors

## Implementation Requirements

### Client API
```rust
use asupersync::{Cx, http::client::HttpClient};
use asupersync::tls::TlsConnectorBuilder;

pub struct Client {
    inner: HttpClient,
}

impl Client {
    pub fn new() -> Result<Self, Error> {
        // Create TLS connector with native roots
        let tls = TlsConnectorBuilder::new()
            .with_native_roots()?
            .build()?;
        let inner = HttpClient::new(tls);
        Ok(Self { inner })
    }
    
    pub fn request(&self, method: Method, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder {
        RequestBuilder::new(self, method, url)
    }
    
    pub fn get(&self, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder { ... }
    pub fn post(&self, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder { ... }
}
```

### RequestBuilder API
```rust
impl RequestBuilder {
    pub fn header(mut self, key: &str, value: &str) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn json<T: Serialize>(mut self, body: &T) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn body(mut self, bytes: Vec<u8>) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn timeout(mut self, duration: Duration) -> Self { ... }
    
    pub async fn send(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<Response, Error> {
        // Actually send the request using asupersync
    }
}
```

### Response API
```rust
pub struct Response {
    status: StatusCode,
    headers: Headers,
    body: BodyReader,  // Async streaming body
}

impl Response {
    pub fn status(&self) -> StatusCode { ... }
    pub fn headers(&self) -> &Headers { ... }
    pub async fn text(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<String, Error> { ... }
    pub async fn json<T: DeserializeOwned>(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<T, Error> { ... }
    pub fn bytes_stream(self) -> impl Stream<Item = Result<Bytes, Error>> { ... }
}
```

## Key Considerations
1. **TLS**: Use rustls with native root certificates (no openssl)
2. **Timeout**: Support per-request timeout via asupersync Budget
3. **Streaming**: Response body must support async streaming for SSE
4. **Headers**: Support standard headers (Content-Type, Authorization, etc.)
5. **Error handling**: Wrap asupersync errors in pi's Error type

## Testing
- Unit test: Client creation succeeds
- Unit test: RequestBuilder builds correct request
- Integration test: GET request to httpbin.org/get
- Integration test: POST with JSON body
- Integration test: Timeout behavior

## Files
- src/http/client.rs (modify)
- src/http/mod.rs (update re-exports)
- src/error.rs (add HTTP error variants)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Client::new() creates working HTTP client
- [ ] GET/POST requests work
- [ ] JSON body serialization works
- [ ] Response streaming works
- [ ] Timeout respected
- [ ] TLS verification works

 {"id":"bd-ah1","title":"Implement session persistence cycle tests","description":"# Implement session persistence cycle tests

## Goal
Test the full session lifecycle: create → save → reload → verify integrity, with detailed logs.

## Background
Session persistence involves:
- JSONL file format (one JSON object per line)
- Header with metadata (version, cwd, timestamp)
- Message entries with parent/child relationships
- Tree structure for conversation branching
- File locking for concurrent access

Current tests cover serialization but not full I/O cycles.

## Runtime + Determinism
- Do not introduce new tokio-only tests; use `#[asupersync::test]` or a runtime wrapper.
- Use temp dirs and deterministic paths; avoid asserting exact timestamps.

## Logging Requirements
- Use TestLogger (bd-3ml).
- Log session path, header fields, message IDs, tree shape, and file sizes.
- On failure, dump the JSONL contents (redacted) and parsed header.

## Test Categories

### 1. Basic Persistence Cycle Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_session_save_and_reload() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/project/path");
    session.add_user_message("Hello");
    session.add_assistant_message("Hi there!");

    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    assert_eq!(loaded.messages().len(), 2);
}
```

### 2. Tree Structure Persistence Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_branching_persists() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/project");
    let root_id = session.add_user_message("Root");
    session.add_assistant_message("Branch A");

    session.navigate_to(root_id.clone());
    session.add_assistant_message("Branch B");

    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    let children = loaded.children_of(&root_id);
    assert_eq!(children.len(), 2);
}
```

### 3. Header Metadata Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_header_metadata_preserved() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/my/project/path");
    session.set_provider("anthropic");
    session.set_model("claude-opus-4");
    session.set_thinking_level(ThinkingLevel::High);
    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    assert_eq!(loaded.cwd(), "/my/project/path");
}
```

### 4. Entry Type Persistence Tests
- ModelChange entries
- ThinkingLevel entries
- Compaction entries
- Tool invocation entries (if serialized)

### 5. Error Handling Tests
- Missing file
- Corrupted JSONL
- Partial corruption (valid header + bad line)

### 6. Concurrent Access Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_concurrent_writes_locked() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let session1 = Session::new("/project");
    session1.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let path = session_path.clone();
    let handle1 = spawn(async move {
        let mut s = Session::load(&path).await.unwrap();
        s.add_user_message("From task 1");
        s.persist(&path).await
    });

    let path = session_path.clone();
    let handle2 = spawn(async move {
        let mut s = Session::load(&path).await.unwrap();
        s.add_user_message("From task 2");
        s.persist(&path).await
    });

    let (r1, r2) = join(handle1, handle2);
    assert!(r1.unwrap().is_ok() || r2.unwrap().is_ok());
}
```

### 7. Directory Structure Tests
- Persist creates nested directories when needed

## Dependencies
None (uses existing session module)

## Files

 {"id":"bd-tg4w","title":"Task: Create EventDispatcher and event type definitions","description":"# Task: Create EventDispatcher and Event Type Definitions

## Objective

Define the event dispatcher infrastructure and all event types that can be dispatched to extensions.

## Implementation

### Event Type Enum

```rust
/// Events that can be dispatched to extension handlers.
/// 
/// Each event corresponds to a lifecycle point in the agent
/// where extensions can observe or intercept behavior.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize)]
#[serde(tag = "type", rename_all = "snake_case")]
pub enum ExtensionEvent {
    /// Agent startup (once per session)
    Startup {
        version: String,
        session_file: Option<String>,
    },
    
    /// Before first API call in a run
    AgentStart {
        session_id: String,
    },
    
    /// After agent loop ends
    AgentEnd {
        session_id: String,
        messages: Vec<Message>,
        error: Option<String>,
    },
    
    /// Before provider.stream() call
    TurnStart {
        session_id: String,
        turn_index: usize,
    },
    
    /// After response processed
    TurnEnd {
        session_id: String,
        turn_index: usize,
        message: AssistantMessage,
        tool_results: Vec<ToolResultMessage>,
    },
    
    /// Before tool execution (can block)
    ToolCall {
        tool_name: String,
        tool_call_id: String,
        input: serde_json::Value,
    },
    
    /// After tool execution (can modify result)
    ToolResult {
        tool_name: String,
        tool_call_id: String,
        input: serde_json::Value,
        content: Vec<ContentBlock>,
        details: Option<serde_json::Value>,
        is_error: bool,
    },
    
    /// Before session switch (can cancel)
    SessionBeforeSwitch {
        current_session: Option<String>,
        target_session: String,
    },
    
    /// Before session fork (can cancel)
    SessionBeforeFork {
        current_session: Option<String>,
        fork_entry_id: String,
    },
    
    /// Before processing user input (can transform)
    Input {
        content: String,
        attachments: Vec<Attachment>,
    },
}
```

### Event Result Types

```rust
/// Result from a tool_call event handler.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default, Deserialize)]
pub struct ToolCallEventResult {
    /// If true, block tool execution
    #[serde(default)]
    pub block: bool,
    
    /// Reason for blocking (shown to user)
    pub reason: Option<String>,
}

/// Result from a tool_result event handler.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Deserialize)]
pub struct ToolResultEventResult {
    /// Modified content (if None, use original)
    pub content: Option<Vec<ContentBlock>>,
    
    /// Modified details (if None, use original)
    pub details: Option<serde_json::Value>,
}

/// Result from input event handler.
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Deserialize)]
pub struct InputEventResult {
    /// Transformed content (if None, use original)
    pub content: Option<String>,
    
    /// If true, block processing
    #[serde(default)]
    pub block: bool,
    
    /// Reason for blocking
    pub reason: Option<String>,
}
```

### EventDispatcher Struct

```rust
/// Dispatches events to extension handlers.
pub struct EventDispatcher {
    runtime: Arc<PiJsRuntime>,
}

impl EventDispatcher {
    pub fn new(runtime: Arc<PiJsRuntime>) -> Self {
        Self { runtime }
    }
    
    /// Check if any extensions have handlers for an event.
    pub fn has_handlers(&self, event_name: &str) -> bool {
        self.runtime.has_event_handlers(event_name)
    }
    
    /// Dispatch an event and wait for all handlers to complete.
    pub async fn dispatch<R>(&self, event: ExtensionEvent) -> Result<Option<R>>
    where
        R: for<'de> Deserialize<'de>,
    {
        let event_name = event.event_name();
        let event_data = serde_json::to_value(&event)?;
        
        self.runtime.dispatch_event(event_name, event_data).await
    }
}

impl ExtensionEvent {
    /// Get the event name for dispatch.
    pub fn event_name(&self) -> &'static str {
        match self {
            Self::Startup { .. } => "startup",
            Self::AgentStart { .. } => "agent_start",
            Self::AgentEnd { .. } => "agent_end",
            Self::TurnStart { .. } => "turn_start",
            Self::TurnEnd { .. } => "turn_end",
            Self::ToolCall { .. } => "tool_call",
            Self::ToolResult { .. } => "tool_result",
            Self::SessionBeforeSwitch { .. } => "session_before_switch",
            Self::SessionBeforeFork { .. } => "session_before_fork",
            Self::Input { .. } => "input",
        }
    }
}
```

 {"id":"bd-tyql","title":"Task: Implement input event dispatch","description":"# Task: Implement Input Event Dispatch

## Objective

Dispatch the input event before processing user messages, allowing extensions to transform or block input.

## Background

The input event fires when the user submits a message. Extensions can:
- Transform input (e.g., expand macros, translate)
- Block inappropriate input
- Add attachments
- Log user activity

## TypeScript Reference

```typescript
// Before processing user input
if (runner.hasHandlers('input')) {
    const inputResult = await runner.emit({
        type: 'input',
        content: userMessage.content,
        attachments: userMessage.attachments,
    });
    
    if (inputResult?.block) {
        // Show message and don't process
        console.log(inputResult.reason || 'Input blocked');
        return;
    }
    
    // Use transformed content if provided
    if (inputResult?.content) {
        userMessage.content = inputResult.content;
    }
}
```

## Implementation

### In Interactive Input Handler

```rust
impl Agent {
    async fn process_user_input(
        &mut self,
        content: String,
        attachments: Vec<Attachment>,
        event_dispatcher: Option<&EventDispatcher>,
    ) -> Result<Option<UserMessage>> {
        // Dispatch input event
        if let Some(dispatcher) = event_dispatcher {
            if dispatcher.has_handlers("input") {
                let event = ExtensionEvent::Input {
                    content: content.clone(),
                    attachments: attachments.clone(),
                };
                
                if let Some(result) = dispatcher.dispatch::<InputEventResult>(event).await? {
                    // Check for blocking
                    if result.block {
                        let reason = result.reason.unwrap_or_else(||
                            "Input blocked by extension".to_string()
                        );
                        self.display_message(&reason)?;
                        return Ok(None);
                    }
                    
                    // Use transformed content if provided
                    let final_content = result.content.unwrap_or(content);
                    
                    return Ok(Some(UserMessage {
                        content: final_content,
                        attachments,
                    }));
                }
            }
        }
        
        Ok(Some(UserMessage { content, attachments }))
    }
}
```
